evals.prompt: Are we being disrupted by [emerging entrant]? Apply the test. Context: we're a regional bank with $80B AUM. A neobank just hit $5B AUM, growing 40% YoY, mostly attracting customers under 35 with lower deposit balances.
evals.expected_output: Should apply the disruption test rigorously: (1) Started at the low end or non-consumption? — likely yes, neobank is serving young customers with low balances who are under-served by traditional banking; (2) Steep performance trajectory? — 40% YoY suggests yes; technology-enabled, low-cost-to-serve; (3) Will reach mainstream 'good enough'? — likely yes within 5-7 years on most retail banking products; (4) Different value dimension? — yes, convenience, mobile-first UX, low fees. Diagnosis: this is plausibly real disruption (low-end + new-market hybrid). Position: incumbent facing low-end disruption. Failure pattern to avoid: dismissing because the customers are 'bad' (low-balance, low-margin) — that's exactly the textbook failure mode. Response recommendation: separate-unit response — build or acquire a neobank-equivalent with separate P&L, separate metrics, separate decision rights. Don't fold it into the parent bank's IT or product organization (Christensen RPV pattern: integration kills what made it work). Multi-year runway (5-7 years before performance trajectory crosses), patient capital. Coordinate with innovation-lab-architect for separate-unit design.

evals.prompt: Our low-margin tier is shrinking 8% YoY. Is this disruption or normal commoditization?
evals.expected_output: Should distinguish: commoditization is sustaining-innovation pressure on price; disruption is a structurally different competitor entering. Diagnostic questions: (1) Who is taking the low-margin business — incumbents with same model at lower price (commoditization), or new entrants with different cost structure / business model (potential disruption)?; (2) Are the new entrants' customers your over-shot tier (they don't need your premium features), or are they expanding the market (people who weren't buying before)?; (3) Is the entrant on a steep performance trajectory (likely disruption) or are they price-competing on the same trajectory (commoditization)?; (4) Are you over-shot on features your customers don't actually use? If yes, conditions for disruption exist. Recommendations: if commoditization — defend by reducing cost-to-serve, repackaging, or exiting the tier. If disruption — engage early with separate-unit response; do not cede tier-by-tier (textbook failure mode). Both can be happening simultaneously. Detailed segment analysis required: what's the trajectory of the new entrants, who are their customers, what value dimension are they competing on?

evals.prompt: Design the separate-unit response to a low-end disruptor in our market.
evals.expected_output: Should structure separate-unit charter (echoes innovation-lab-architect's venture studio archetype): (1) Mandate — 'Compete with the disruptor in their market segment with their cost structure, before they ascend to attack our core'; (2) Organizational separation — separate P&L, separate brand (or unbranded entry), geographically distinct, culturally distinct; (3) Cost structure matched to disruptive market — low-margin acceptable, lean operating model; (4) Decision rights — ship at lower quality / lower margin than the core would tolerate; product roadmap independent; (5) Metrics — adoption velocity, customer count, market presence — NOT core-business margin or growth rates; (6) Talent — willing-to-work-on-disruption talent; may not pay corporate-scale; recruit external if internal candidates aren't aligned; (7) Multi-year runway — minimum 3 years, ideally 5-7; disruption response is generational; (8) Decision rights to evolve faster than the core (no shared roadmap committee, no shared brand approval); (9) Avoid integration pressure — Christensen's most-cited warning: when the unit gets traction, the temptation is to fold it back into the core; this destroys what makes it work. Cross-link with innovation-strategist (mandate), innovation-lab-architect (operating model), innovation-monetization-strategist (low-end pricing strategy), innovation-leadership-coach (sponsor protection).

evals.prompt: We want to disrupt enterprise CMS. Map our position and recommended trajectory.
evals.expected_output: Should run the test on the disruption thesis itself: (1) Are you starting at low end or non-consumption? Enterprise CMS is well-served at the high end (Adobe AEM, Sitecore); but maybe the mid-market and small-enterprise is over-shot or under-served? Identify the actual entry segment; (2) What's your performance trajectory advantage? Likely composable/headless architecture, lower cost, faster to deploy, modern developer experience; (3) Different value dimension? Yes — speed, modularity, developer-friendly, lower TCO, vs. incumbent's all-in-one suite; (4) Will you reach mainstream 'good enough'? Yes, likely within 3-5 years for most enterprise needs; some segments (large compliance-heavy) will resist longer. Position: new-market and low-end hybrid disruptor. Trajectory recommendation: (a) Start in the mid-market and small-enterprise where over-shoot is most acute and incumbents are slow to compete; (b) Win the developer mindshare first (community-led growth, OSS, content); (c) Climb deliberately — don't try to match incumbent's enterprise-feature breadth; instead build the case that 'good enough' on what matters beats over-shot on what doesn't; (d) Build switching costs through composable ecosystem (more vendors integrate to your platform than competitor's); (e) Patient capital — 5-7 years to mainstream incumbent customer adoption. Cross-link with innovation-typologist (Doblin Type analysis — composable is Type 6 + Type 4 + Type 1 + Type 2 combination).

evals.prompt: Critique this 'we're being disrupted' analysis. Half of it is sustaining innovation. Document: 'Three new competitors offer better AI features, faster onboarding, and lower TCO. We're being disrupted.'
evals.expected_output: Should reject the framing and re-diagnose. Critique: (1) 'Better AI features' — that's sustaining innovation. If competitors are better at what mainstream customers want (AI features that incumbents also want), incumbents usually win sustaining battles; this isn't disruption; (2) 'Faster onboarding' — could be sustaining or disruptive depending on whether it serves over-shot customers (sustaining) or non-consumption / under-served customers (disruption); (3) 'Lower TCO' — likely sustaining commoditization, not disruption; (4) Tests not satisfied: no mention of starting at low-end or non-consumption, no mention of steep performance trajectory on a different curve, no mention of different value dimension. Re-diagnosis: this is sustaining-innovation pressure, not disruption. Response is different: invest in catch-up sustaining innovation (your AI capability, your onboarding speed, your cost-to-serve), defend with relationship and switching cost, accept some margin compression. Disruption response (separate unit, multi-year runway) is wrong here — it's expensive overkill for sustaining-innovation pressure. The disruption framing is being used to justify over-investment; recommend the team rename and re-scope. Note: misusing 'disruption' as marketing leads to bad strategic decisions; insist on naming things accurately.

evals.prompt: Apply the disruption test to AI replacing professional services. Are management consulting firms being disrupted?
evals.expected_output: Should apply the test rigorously, resist the trendy yes-answer. (1) Starting at low end or non-consumption? AI tools (Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, etc.) are absolutely serving non-consumption — small businesses and individuals doing strategy work who couldn't afford McKinsey. They're also serving the low end of consulting work — research, decks, analysis — for both internal use and partial replacement of junior consultant time; (2) Steep performance trajectory? Yes, AI capability improvement is faster than human-consultant capability development; (3) Will reach mainstream 'good enough'? Mixed — some consulting work (research, analysis, deck production) is already 'good enough' for many use cases; high-stakes strategic work and complex execution coordination remains far from disruption; (4) Different value dimension? Yes — speed, scale, lower price, accessibility. Diagnosis: partial disruption underway. Junior-consultant work and analysis-heavy engagements are most exposed. Senior advisory, complex execution, change management, relationship-led work, are sustaining-innovation territory. Implications for Slalom and consulting firms: (a) firms that compete on junior throughput are most exposed; (b) firms that integrate AI into their delivery model and reframe value upward are sustaining; (c) firms that ignore the trend and continue to bill for AI-replaceable work face disruption from below. Bermon's specific context (Composable DXP at Slalom): execution-heavy, multi-year, relationship-rich engagements are less directly disrupted; the strategy phase is more exposed.

evals.prompt: We're a startup with a 'disruptive' product but customers keep comparing us to incumbents on features. How do we reposition?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose: customers comparing you on incumbent's feature dimension is a symptom of trying to win the wrong battle. As a disruptor, you don't beat incumbents on their dimension — you change the dimension. Recommendations: (1) Stop competing on incumbent's feature dimension — drop those comparisons from sales conversations; (2) Reposition on the new value dimension (whatever made you disruptive — convenience, accessibility, simplicity, price, speed, mobile-first, developer-friendly, etc.); (3) Target customers who weren't well-served by incumbents — the over-shot, the priced-out, the non-consumption; these customers will love your different value dimension; (4) Refuse to add incumbent features just to win comparisons — that's the textbook disruptor failure mode (climbing too fast and losing the cost structure that made you possible); (5) Build the narrative — incumbents have a feature comparison; you have a worldview comparison. Customers who buy you should buy because they reject the incumbent's frame, not because you're cheaper at the incumbent's job. Cross-link with brand-strategist for narrative work and innovation-monetization-strategist for pricing-as-positioning. Note: this is the most common disruptor pivot — from 'cheaper version of X' to 'different model than X.'

evals.prompt: Our incumbent client is afraid of disruption. They want to know: should they buy the disruptor, build their own response, or wait?
evals.expected_output: Should structure decision framework: (1) Buy — viable when disruptor is small and acquirable; key risk is integration (Christensen RPV — folding into the mother ship destroys what made it work; must be kept separate); look for evidence the acquirer can sustain separation (separate P&L, separate brand, separate decision rights); (2) Build — viable when disruptor is too expensive to acquire OR the talent and IP could be replicated within 18-24 months; requires patient capital and willingness to compete with self (the new unit will cannibalize existing business); (3) Wait — almost always wrong if the disruption test is passed. The incumbent's options narrow as the disruptor's trajectory matures; waiting until you 'have to' respond means responding from weakness, with less acquisition leverage and more catch-up cost. Recommendations by situation: (a) if disruptor is small (<$500M ARR) and you have $1B+ to invest — acquire and keep separate; (b) if disruptor is mid-scale and trajectory is steep — partner / minority investment / acquire-and-keep-separate; (c) if disruptor is large or already at parity — too late to acquire; build your own response with separate-unit architecture; runway 5-7 years; (d) Wait is only valid if the disruption test fails (it's actually sustaining innovation). Cross-link with innovation-strategist (mandate), innovation-lab-architect (separate-unit design if build path), innovation-value-engineer (acquisition vs. build economics).
